text: Hello, Adam Roth from LGN presenting another robotic sigmoid colectomy for adenocarcinoma of the mid sigmoid colon. This case was pretty tough and it was all related to the patient's BMI and body habitus in general. He was a BMI of forty-one but it was all in his belly. So as you'll see there's just a tremendous amount of visceral fat and I had trouble with retraction and even elevating the colon to put tension on the mesentery without things tearing. And then of course keeping the small bowel up out of the pelvis and away from the area that you're trying to dissect when you're doing the medial to lateral is challenging too as you will see. So here I'm just kind of trying to restore normal anatomy. Mark Solomon would say that that's kind of the first step of the operation I think. And then like I said in the other videos, I do like to do some of the lateral mobilization, identify that ureter even laterally before heading to the medial to lateral dissection, especially in a case like this where the mesenteric fat is so abundant.

The surgeon provides a first view of the contracted tumor located in the middle of the sigmoid colon. The surrounding area shows a significant amount of visceral fat, complicating retraction efforts. The assistant uses the fourth robotic arm to elevate the rectum, applying tension on the rectal mesentery to facilitate the medial to lateral dissection.

The surgeon connects the lateral and medial dissections, ensuring the ureter is identified and preserved. The surgical field shows the proximal resection margin, with the surgeon maneuvering around the area without yet taking the vessels.

The surgeon flips the colon medially and navigates through the substantial mesenteric fat to access the proximal colon. The inferior mesenteric artery is ligated, and the surgeon progresses towards the proximal resection margin, ensuring not to lose track of the vessels amidst the dense fat.

The surgeon selects the distal resection margin at the rectosigmoid junction, ensuring the dissection is lower than the transection point to facilitate anastomosis. The mesentery is meticulously cleaned to prevent interference with the staple line. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to confirm perfusion at the proximal and distal ends. A fan steel incision is made to extract the specimen, followed by placing the anvil and completing the anastomosis with assistance through a gel port. A leak test is performed using a rigid proctoscope to ensure the anastomosis is adequately open.

All right, well, just going to clean up here a little bit, bring the momentum back down on top of the bowel. So I really appreciate you watching. The things I'm looking to kind of get from any feedback here would be, how do you deal with these people that have just so much intra-abdominal fat? The colon is very heavy. The mesentery is very thick. So what instruments do you use to retract? What do you grab when you're retracting? How do you utilize your assistant better than I did? And then keeping the small bowel up out of the pelvis. Those things would be really helpful if you have any advice. Thanks again.
